title: Thrombophilia
body_html:
Definition
Thrombophilia refers to a predisposition to venous thromboembolism (VTE) due to inherited or acquired prothrombotic factors.

Causes

InheritedAcquired
• Factor V Leiden (most common)
• Prothrombin gene mutation (G20210A)
• Protein C deficiency
• Protein S deficiency
• Antithrombin deficiency (highest risk, but rare)		• Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)
• Malignancy
• Pregnancy, oestrogen use
• Myeloproliferative disorders
• Nephrotic syndrome

Classification of inherited thrombophilia
Inherited thrombophilia may be classed as type 1 or type 2 where:

  • Type 1 defects are the result of a deficiency of anticoagulant proteins. These are usually more serious and more rare e.g. antithrombin III and protein C and S deficiencies. These are usually the result of a deficiency of anticoagulants.
  • Type 2 defects are the result of producing dysfunctional anticoagulant proteins, this results in normal levels of said protein but overall effect is reduced, with resulting overproduction of clotting factors. Type 2 defects are more common and relatively mild i.e Factor V Leiden (heterozyous) and Prothrombin G20210A mutations.


Inherited thrombophiliaPrevalance %Relative risk of VTE
Factor V Leiden4-5%heterozygotes 3-8 / homozygotes 50-80
Prothrombin G20210a2.0%3
Protein C deficiency0.2% (estimated)10-15
Protein S deficiency0.03-0.13% (estimated)10
Antithrombin deficiency0.02-0.06%25-50
Dysfibrinogenaemiararevariable

Clinical Features
Inherited causes:
  • Recurrent VTE
  • VTE at young age (<50)
  • Family history
  • VTE in unusual sites (e.g., portal, cerebral veins)
Acquired causes:
  • VTE ± arterial thrombosis (APS)
  • Pregnancy morbidity (Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is a recognised cause of recurrent miscarriage, IUGR & pre-eclampsia)
  • Thrombosis with cancer, surgery, immobility

Investigation & screening
  • Universal screening is not currently recommended
  • Selective testing based on history is more cost-effective than universal screening.
  • Avoid testing during acute events or while on anticoagulants
  • In pregnant women, APS testing should be done outside pregnancy

Screening
Asymptomatic relatives of patients with type 2 thrombophilia's do not need investigation.

Management
Anticoagulation for acute episodes, possibly long-term depending on risk (In APS warfarin is favoured over DOACs)

Prophylaxis during high-risk periods e.g. surgery, immobility or pregnancy.

Avoid triggers such as oestrogen-containing contraception and HRT when thrombophilia is present.

Prognosis
  • VTE risk depends on thrombophilia type plus patient-specific and environmental factors.
  • Many with heritable thrombophilia never develop thrombosis
  • Testing may cause anxiety & counseling is advised

Key Points
  • Factor V Leiden is the most prevalent cause of congenital thrombophilia and if the condition is homozygous carries the highest relative VTE risk (see table)
  • Recurrent miscarriage is associated with Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)
  • Patients with blood group O have a 2-4 fold reduction in relative risk of VTE than those with non O blood groups. Lower factor VIII and vWF in these patients is protective.
  • Of note two or more conditions can coexist and carry very high relative risks
  • Congenital (inherited) thrombophilias can be type 1 or type 2.
NCBI10
Hypercoagulability


BSCH10
Testing for heritable thrombophilias
